title: STANDARD FOR TANNIA 1 CXS 224-2001 Adopted in 2001. Amended in 2005, 2011.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR TANNIA 1 CXS 224-2001 Adopted in 2001. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document heading for the Codex Standard for Tannia (CXS 224-2001, adopted 2001; amended 2005, 2011) listing common regional names (heading, no substantive provisions).

Defines the scope: applies to tubercles of commercial lilac tannia (Xanthosoma violaceum) and white tannia (Xanthosoma sagittifolium) supplied fresh to the consumer after preparation and packaging, and excludes tannias intended for industrial processing.

Establishes general quality provisions for tannias, including minimum requirements (whole, sound, clean, practically pest-free and pest-damage-free, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, firm, practically free of mechanical damage/bruising and sprouting, physiologically developed and able to withstand transport) and classification into three classes (Extra, I, II) with class-specific defect allowances.

Specifies minimum quality requirements for all classes of tannias: must be whole, sound (no rotting), clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, firm, practically free of mechanical damage/bruising and sprouting, physiologically mature, and in a condition to withstand transport and arrive satisfactorily.

Defines classification into three classes—'Extra', Class I and Class II—with progressively looser allowances for superficial defects while maintaining minimum requirements and stipulating that defects must not affect the flesh, and specifying percentage limits for scarring/scraped areas by class.

'Extra' Class: tannias must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety and free of defects except very slight superficial defects that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation in the package.

Class I: tannias of good quality and characteristic of the variety where slight defects are allowed—slight shape defects, scarring up to 20% of surface area and scraped areas up to 20%—provided defects do not affect flesh, appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class II: includes tannias meeting the minimum requirements but not higher-class criteria, permitting defects in shape and scarring or scraped areas up to 30% of surface area, provided the produce retains essential characteristics and defects do not affect the flesh.

Size is determined by weight and dimensional limits with three size codes: A (150–249 g), B (250–349 g) and C (350–450 g), each with length between 100–300 mm and diameter 45–70 mm measured as specified.

Sets tolerances for quality and size: quality tolerances allow specific percentages by number or weight of produce not meeting class requirements (Extra 5% meeting Class I; Class I 10% meeting Class II; Class II 10% that may fail class/minimum except produce unfit due to rotting), and size tolerances permit 10% by number or weight of tannias of the size immediately above or below the declared size.

Details quality tolerances by class: Extra allows 5% by number/weight not meeting Extra but meeting Class I or within that class's tolerances; Class I allows 10% not meeting Class I but meeting Class II or within its tolerances; Class II allows 10% that satisfy neither the class nor the minimum requirements, excluding produce unfit for consumption.

Extra class quality tolerance: up to 5% by number or weight of tannias in a package may fail Extra-class requirements provided they meet Class I requirements or, exceptionally, fall within that class's tolerances.

Class I quality tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of tannias in a package may fail Class I requirements provided they meet Class II requirements or, exceptionally, fall within that class's tolerances.

Class II quality tolerance: up to 10% by number or weight of tannias in a package may fail both Class II and minimum requirements, except for produce affected by rotting or otherwise unfit for consumption.
Size tolerances: for all classes, up to 10% by number or weight of tannias in a package may correspond to the size immediately above and/or below the size indicated on the package.

Sets presentation requirements: packages must be uniform by origin/variety/commercial type/quality/size with the visible contents representative, and packaging must protect the produce using new/clean (or food-grade recycled) materials and containers meeting hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards in line with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport (CXC 44-1995).
Uniformity requirement: each package must contain only tannias of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, and the visible part of the contents must represent the entire package.

Packaging requirements: tannias must be packed to protect the produce, using materials that are new, clean and of suitable quality (printing/labels must use non-toxic ink/glue), and packed in compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Container description: containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength requirements to ensure safe handling, shipping and preservation and be free of foreign matter and smells, with footnotes permitting smells from compliant conservation agents and the use of food-grade recycled materials.

Labelling requirements: consumer packages must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) plus specific provisions (e.g., declare produce name when not visible), and non-retail containers must bear legible identification (exporter/packer/dispatcher), nature and origin of produce, commercial identification (type, class, size, optional net weight) and may carry an official inspection mark.
Section heading introducing additional labelling provisions for consumer packages in addition to the General Standard (CXS 1-1985); the excerpt itself contains no standalone labelling particulars.
If the produce is not visible from the outside, each package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may also show the variety and/or commercial type.
Each non‑retail package must bear specified particulars grouped on the same side, legibly and indelibly marked, visible from the outside or provided in accompanying documents.
Label must include the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher; an identification code is optional.
If the contents are not visible, the package must indicate the name of the produce and may optionally indicate the variety and/or commercial type.
Packages must state the country of origin and may optionally specify the district or a national, regional or local place name.
Commercial identification must indicate type (e.g., white or lilac), class, size (size code or minimum and maximum weight in grams) and may include net weight.
Non‑substantive heading noting an optional official inspection mark is applicable, with no further details provided.
The produce must comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Preparation and handling are recommended to follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and the produce should meet any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21‑1997.
